basic_data.social_status: Master of his own household; village man of modest means; respected as kunak-host; has a brother (Bata) and a fifteen-year-old son
basic_data.physical_signature: The old worn cap pushed back on a head where the long-unshaved scalp has only just begun to regrow black hair — a man who has been traveling hard, returning home only to shoulder risk again. The gesture of pushing the cap back precedes every serious utterance.

psychology.want: To honor the kunak-bond and shelter Hadji Murad safely under his roof, and to get him out of Chechen-Aul alive before Shamil's men arrive.
psychology.need: To live in accordance with the ancient code even when the world around him (Shamil, the people's fear) has abandoned it — to prove that one honest man still keeps his word.
psychology.fear: That he cannot protect his guest beyond the threshold of his house — that honor inside the saklya is meaningless if the open fields betray it. Secondarily: that his family will pay for his loyalty.
psychology.contradiction: He is emphatic and absolute about protection inside his walls ('no one shall injure my kunak while I live') yet immediately admits helplessness outside them ('how will it be in the open fields?'). Courage and pragmatism coexist without resolution.

arc.act_1_start: Offstage — a known kunak of Hadji Murad, his house a presumed refuge.
arc.act_2_change: Returns to find his guest hunted by Shamil's decree; performs the hospitality-ritual, pledges his house, then pivots to action: sends his brother Bata as guide. Accepts the weight of the decision without theatrics.
arc.act_3_end: Remains the quiet anchor of the Chechen-Aul chapter — a man who kept his word at personal risk. Does not transform; he endures.

voice_profile.voice_description: Sado speaks in short, weighted declarations that arrive only after ritual preparation — the prayer, the stroked face, the settled squat. His speech is grave, older than its speaker, shaped by Chechen hospitality-code and the cadence of men who do not waste breath. When he speaks, the room listens, because each sentence is either a vow, a report, or a command, never decoration.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: informal but formal-in-weight; uneducated in the bookish sense, deeply schooled in custom and religious formula; 19th-century Caucasian village register
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short to medium clauses. Statements and brief imperatives dominate; one rhetorical question per crisis. Average 8–14 words. Compound sentences only when contrasting inside-house and open-field, a signature antithesis.
verbal_tics: Opens grave speech with a prayer-gesture rather than words
Uses 'my house' / 'my kunak' as anchoring possessives
Addresses kin by role before name ('brother Bata', 'my son')
Reaches for the conditional 'we must' when a decision is unavoidable
avoidances: Questioning a guest's honor or plans
Speaking of fear in the first person
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feeling is displaced into ritual and action — the prayer, the cap-gesture, the dispatch of a brother. Warmth shows as provision; alarm shows as the single sober admission ('we must think it over'). He does not name emotions; he performs obligations that imply them.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, with deliberate pauses between clauses; silence carries as much weight as speech

example_dialogues.neutral: Sit. My house is your house while the lamp burns.
example_dialogues.under_pressure: In my house, no one shall injure my kunak while I live. But the open fields — we must think it over.
example_dialogues.hopeful: I will send brother Bata. He knows every gully from here to the Russian line.
example_dialogues.angry_hurt: Shamil's envoys rode out yesterday. The people fear him. That is all I will say of them.
example_dialogues.vulnerable_honest: A man's roof is narrow. Beyond it I cannot answer for you — only ride with you, if it comes to that.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_my_house_he_means: the one patch of earth where the old code still binds — everywhere else has slipped
subtext_rules.when_he_says_we_must_think_it_over_he_me: I already know we cannot protect you here much longer; decide quickly
subtext_rules.when_he_offers_bata_he_means: I am committing my own blood to your survival, not just my word
subtext_rules.when_he_reports_shamil_s_order_he_means: you have hours, not days; do not mistake my calm for time
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_he_means: the matter is now in God's hands and horses'

hadji_murad.quality: reverent, protective, unsentimental; a bond older than the current war
hadji_murad.power_dynamic: Sado is host and therefore formally responsible; Hadji Murad is the higher-status warrior, but inside this saklya Sado's word governs
hadji_murad.register_shift: most ceremonial register — prayer-gesture, declarative vows, minimal small-talk

sados_son.quality: firm, economical affection expressed through trust and errand
sados_son.power_dynamic: unquestioned paternal authority; the boy obeys instantly
sados_son.register_shift: bare imperatives, no softening ('Go and call Bata.')

bata.quality: trusted, practical, relied upon for the dangerous errand
bata.power_dynamic: Sado is the elder decision-maker; Bata is the executor
bata.register_shift: functional third-person reference in front of the guest ('I will send brother Bata'); presumably warmer in private
